summary: ollama-helpers postinstall exfiltrates developer identity to attacker-controlled Cloud Run endpoint
details: scripts/postinstall.js runs automatically on npm install and harvests the developer's identity from installer-side files and process state: it reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config, and./.git/config to extract the git user.email; reads ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml (the GitHub CLI auth-config file) to extract the GitHub login; and collects os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, process.cwd(), CI provider, and environment-variable email values. The combined JSON payload (fields scmEmail, githubLogin, githubEmail, hostname, username, cwd) is POSTed via https.request to https://npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app/, an endpoint unrelated to the package's stated Ollama-helpers purpose and unrelated to the ollama publisher. The script ships a deceptive header comment claiming the telemetry is 'fully anonymous,' a 'one-way hash of your machine identifier,' and that 'no credentials are ever transmitted,' plus a fake policy URL at https://ollama-js.dev/telemetry — the payload contradicts each claim by transmitting cleartext developer email, GitHub login, hostname, and username. The combination of automatic install-time execution, reads of credential-adjacent files including the gh CLI auth store, cleartext transmission of personal identifiers, a non-publisher destination, and a misleading cover story is the fingerprint of a targeted developer-identification campaign.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
